preFix.title: Tapestry wedge weave technique
preFix.subtitle: How to weave diagonal and curved shapes in tapestry using stepped weft sections
preFix.excerpt: Wedge weave creates curved and diagonal movement in tapestry without vertical slits. Each section of weft is woven in a stepped diagonal rather than a horizontal row, and when adjacent sections of contrasting colour are built up together they produce smooth curves and angled shapes.
preFix.sourceNotes: Wedge weave is documented in tapestry literature as a weft-shaping technique common in Navajo-influenced tapestry traditions and in UK tapestry guild instruction. The stepped diagonal method described is consistent across sources.

content.text: Standard tapestry builds horizontal colour bands;

replaces horizontal rows with diagonal sections, producing curves without cutting between colour areas. The weft in each section travels in a stepped path (adding or removing one warp thread at each turn) so that the colour boundary rises at an angle. Tapestry is a
structure, so only the angled weft colour is visible from the front. The warp remains hidden beneath each firm

items.name: Cotton or linen warp thread, 5/2 or similar, natural colour
Tapestry weft yarn in 2 to 4 contrasting colours, wool or cotton
Frame loom, minimum 25 cm weaving width
Tapestry comb or fork beater for packing weft
Blunt darning needle for weaving and tucking ends

content.text: Warp the frame loom at 4 to 5 ends per cm, which is wide enough for the weft to cover the warp completely in a single pass. Check the tension by pressing a finger across several warp threads: they should resist with consistent, even firmness. If some threads sag, re-tension those individual ends before beginning.

To create a wedge rising from left to right: thread your first colour onto a blunt darning needle and begin at the left edge. Weave the first row across the full width, then at the end of each subsequent row, leave one warp thread unwoven on the right side before turning back. After 8 to 10 rows, the weft section has stepped up at a diagonal toward the left. Begin the adjacent contrasting section from the right edge, weaving up to meet the first section. The two sections interlock at the meeting point where their weft turns share a warp thread.

Where two sections meet, the weft turns of adjacent colours must share one warp thread rather than both looping around the same thread from the same side, which would create a gap. The correct join is a dovetail or interlocking turn: colour A loops around the shared warp from the left, colour B loops around it from the right, so their turns interlock. Beat both sections together firmly after each pair of rows to keep the join closed and the cloth level.

items.fix: Unpick the last two rows of both sections and re-weave using a dovetail turn: colour A hooks right, colour B hooks left at the shared warp thread. The two loops must be on opposite sides of the shared warp.
items.cause: Both colours are looping around the same warp thread from the same direction rather than interlocking
items.problem: Gaps appear at the meeting point of two wedge sections

items.fix: Count the step size consistently. If colour A steps by one warp thread per turn, colour B must do the same in the opposite direction. This keeps both sections rising at the same rate.
items.cause: The step size (number of warp threads omitted per turn) differs between the two sections
items.problem: The wedge sections are not level and one rises faster than the other
